content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 5, 2014

Elizabeth Siggins
Californians for Safety and Justice
1970 Broadway Ste 260
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-14-006

Dear Ms. Siggins:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment provisions. Additionally, the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops 71.)

QUESTION

Does the one-year ban prevent you from attending meetings with your former state employer on behalf of your new, private employer?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The types of communications you intend to have with your former employer are among those that are subject to the one-year ban.

FACTS

You are a former Senior Policy Advisor, a designated position, with the California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation ("CDCR"). You permanently left this position on May 31, 2013, and you now work as the director for a project with Californians for Safety and

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-14-006
Page No. 2

Justice, a non-profit corporation that receives funding from the California Endowment. You are
working specifically on a grant from the California Endowment to assist public safety entities in
implementing the Affordable Care Act ("ACA"). One of these entities is CDCR.

As part of the process, you would attend meetings with CDCR employees regarding such
issues as technical assistance, applying enrollment protocol, and other problem solving as CDCR
attempts to implement the ACA and corresponding state legislation. Your contact with CDCR
staff would be through meetings and phone calls to assist their process in enrolling eligible
individuals for health care insurance.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment restrictions under the Act, colloquially known as the "revolving door" prohibitions.
The first is the "permanent ban" provision, which prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the
State of California if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated while
employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402, Regulation 18741.1.) The permanent ban is
a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which you
participated while you served as a state administrative official. Based on your facts, the
permanent ban does not apply here as there is no judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding.

The second is the "one-year ban" provision, which prohibits certain state employees from
communicating, for compensation, with their former agency for the purpose of influencing
certain administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale
or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406, Regulation 18746.1.) Your facts
implicate the one-year ban, based on your former position with CDCR and your current position
with a non-profit that is advising CDCR for purposes of the ACA. Because there are no factors
indicating that the permanent ban is involved, we address only the one-year ban.

The one-year ban prohibits a state employee from making, for compensation, any formal
or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before her former agency
for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.)
The Act defines administrative action, in part, as "the proposal, drafting, development,
consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or
other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . .." (Section
82002(a).) Additionally, "legislative action" means the drafting, introduction, consideration,
modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other
matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select
committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity.
'Legislative action,' also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill."
(Section 82037.)

File No. A-14-006
Page No. 3

The ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).)

While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).) In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).)

You are advising and educating CDCR on ways that it might efficiently apply the ACA to eligible participants, such as recent parolees. Under the grant obtained by the California Endowment, your non-profit will assist in educating CDCR about the benefits of enrolling participants in the ACA and in developing effective ways of achieving that. While we do not have specific information regarding the types of decisions CDCR will make in response to the information you provide, it does appear as though CDCR will take this information and develop policies, rules, and protocols that could have far-reaching effects. You are therefore considered to be influencing administrative action or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406, Regulation 18746.1.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Heather M. Rowan
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.question:
Does the one•year ban prevent you from attending meetings with your former state 
employer on behalf of your new, private employer?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The types of communications you intend to have with your former employer are 
among those that are subject to the one-year ban.

(Regulation 18746.l(b)(S).) 
You are advising and educating CDCR on ways that it might efficiently apply the ACA
